title: Binary Search
description: The O(log n) algorithm previewed back in Big-O Notation -- cutting a sorted array in half with every comparison instead of scanning linearly.
objectives: Implement binary search on a sorted array
Trace binary search's mid/left/right pointer movement
Understand why binary search requires a sorted array and is O(log n)

sections.text: 1. The Problem: Linear Search is O(n)
Linear search checks every element, one by one -- O(n). In Big-O Notation, we previewed that binary search achieves O(log n) instead, by exploiting one key fact: the array is SORTED.
An algorithm that finds a target in a SORTED array by repeatedly checking the middle element and eliminating half of the remaining search space based on the comparison.

mid = left + (right - left) / 2, NOT (left + right) / 2! Both give the same mathematical result, but (left + right) / 2 can overflow int's range for very large arrays (recall Data Types' int range), while left + (right - left) / 2 avoids this risk entirely. This is a well-known, deliberate defensive habit.

sections.title: Tracing binarySearch({2,5,8,12,16,23,38,45,56,72}, 23)
steps: left=0, right=9: mid=0+(9-0)/2=4. arr[4]=16 < 23 -- search right half: left=5
left=5, right=9: mid=5+(9-5)/2=7. arr[7]=45 > 23 -- search left half: right=6
left=5, right=6: mid=5+(6-5)/2=5. arr[5]=23 -- MATCH! return 5

sections.title: The halving argument
points: Each comparison eliminates HALF of the remaining search space entirely
Starting from n elements: after 1 step, n/2 remain; after 2 steps, n/4; after k steps, n/2^k
The search ends when only 1 element remains -- roughly when n/2^k = 1, meaning k = log2(n)
For n = 1,000,000, log2(n) is about 20 -- just 20 comparisons instead of up to a million

sections.text: 4. Why Binary Search Requires a Sorted Array

arr[mid] < target only tells us to search the right half BECAUSE the array is sorted -- everything to the left of mid is guaranteed to be even smaller. On an unsorted array, this guarantee vanishes completely, and binary search simply doesn't work; you'd need to sort first (see the Sorting lesson), which itself costs time.

This variant doesn't stop at the FIRST match found -- it records the match, then keeps narrowing toward the LEFT (right = mid - 1) to check for an even earlier occurrence of the same value. This pattern -- 'binary search, but keep going in one direction after a match' -- is extremely common in DSA problems built on top of plain binary search.

sections.title: Common Beginner Mistakes
items: Using (left + right) / 2 instead of left + (right - left) / 2, risking integer overflow on very large arrays.
Applying binary search to an unsorted array -- it silently gives wrong answers instead of an obvious error, since the halving logic assumes sorted order.
Off-by-one errors in the loop condition (left <= right, not left < right) or in updating left/right (mid + 1 / mid - 1, not just mid), causing an infinite loop or missing the target.
Forgetting to keep narrowing after a match when searching for the FIRST or LAST occurrence of a duplicated value, instead of returning immediately.

sections.title: Interview Questions

questions.question: What is binary search, and what does it require of its input?
questions.answer: An algorithm that finds a target in a SORTED array by repeatedly checking the middle element and eliminating half the remaining search space based on the comparison. It requires the array to already be sorted -- it does not work correctly on unsorted data.

questions.question: Why is binary search's time complexity O(log n)?
questions.answer: Each comparison eliminates half of the remaining elements, so after k steps only n/2^k elements remain. The search ends when this shrinks to 1 element, which happens after roughly log2(n) steps.

questions.question: Why is left + (right - left) / 2 preferred over (left + right) / 2 for computing mid?
questions.answer: (left + right) can overflow int's maximum value for very large arrays, producing an incorrect (often negative) mid. left + (right - left) / 2 computes the same midpoint without that overflow risk.

sections.thirtySecondAnswer: Binary search finds a target in a SORTED array in O(log n) by repeatedly checking the middle element and eliminating half the remaining search space based on whether the target is smaller or larger -- for a million elements, this needs only about 20 comparisons instead of up to a million with linear search. It requires the array to be sorted, since the halving logic relies on everything left of mid being smaller and everything right being larger. mid is computed as left + (right - left) / 2 rather than (left + right) / 2 to avoid integer overflow on large arrays. Variants of binary search, like finding the first or last occurrence of a duplicated value, keep narrowing in one direction even after finding a match, instead of returning immediately.

sections.title: Trick to Remember
sections.text: Binary search = guessing a number 1-100 by always guessing the MIDDLE and eliminating half the range each time -- 'higher' or 'lower' cuts your work in half, every single guess. Only works because the range is ORDERED.

sections.title: Quick Understanding Check
questions: Why does binary search require a sorted array?
What is binary search's time complexity, and why?
Why is left + (right - left) / 2 safer than (left + right) / 2?
How would you modify binary search to find the FIRST occurrence of a duplicated value?

sections.title: Binary Search
sections.problemStatement: Given a sorted array of integers nums and an integer target, return the index of target if it exists, or -1 otherwise. Write an O(log n) algorithm.
constraints: nums is sorted in ascending order, all unique
hints: This IS the exact binarySearch function from this lesson's Section 2, unmodified.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: Linear scan, checking every element.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Standard binary search, exactly as covered in this lesson.

sections.title: Search Insert Position
sections.problemStatement: Given a sorted array of distinct integers nums and a target value, return the index if the target is found. If not, return the index where it would be inserted to keep the array sorted.
hints: Standard binary search, but instead of returning -1 on a miss, return where left ends up -- that IS the correct insertion point.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: Linear scan for the first index whose value is >= target.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Binary search; when the loop ends without finding target, left has naturally converged to the correct insertion index.

sections.title: Sqrt(x)
sections.problemStatement: Given a non-negative integer x, return the square root of x rounded DOWN to the nearest integer. You must not use any built-in exponent function.
hints: Binary search on the ANSWER itself: search the range [0, x] for the largest value whose square doesn't exceed x.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: Try every integer from 0 upward until its square exceeds x.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Binary search on the range [0, x] for the largest value whose square is <= x -- using long to avoid overflow when squaring.

sections.title: Valid Perfect Square
sections.problemStatement: Given a positive integer num, return true if num is a perfect square, false otherwise. You must not use any built-in exponent function.
hints: Same binary-search-on-the-answer technique as Sqrt(x) -- find the integer square root, then check if squaring it gives back exactly num.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: Try every integer from 1 upward until its square reaches or exceeds num.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Binary search for the integer square root, then verify by squaring it back.

sections.title: Guess Number Higher or Lower
sections.problemStatement: A number is picked between 1 and n. You must guess which number is picked, using a provided API guess(num) that returns -1 if the pick is lower than num, 1 if higher, and 0 if you guessed correctly. Minimize the number of calls.
hints: The guess() API result plays the exact role of the arr[mid] comparison in ordinary binary search -- the 'array' here is just the range of numbers 1 to n, never materialized.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: Guess every number from 1 upward until correct -- valid, but far more calls than necessary.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Binary search over the range [1, n], using guess()'s result exactly like a comparison against a sorted array's middle element.

sections.title: First Bad Version
sections.problemStatement: You are given n versions [1, n], and want to find the first bad one, given that all versions after a bad one are also bad. You are provided an API isBadVersion(version) that returns whether a given version is bad. Minimize the number of calls.

examples.explanation: Versions 4 and 5 are bad; the FIRST bad version is 4.

hints: This is exactly this course's firstOccurrence pattern from the Binary Search lesson's Section 5, but the 'array' being searched is conceptual (isBadVersion) rather than a real array.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: Check every version starting from 1 until the first bad one is found.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Binary search for the leftmost 'bad' version -- whenever isBadVersion(mid) is true, it might still not be the FIRST bad one, so search left; otherwise search right.

sections.title: Find First and Last Position of Element in Sorted Array
sections.problemStatement: Given an array of integers nums sorted in ascending order, find the starting and ending position of a given target value. If not found, return [-1, -1]. Must run in O(log n).
hints: Run this lesson's firstOccurrence twice: once as-is for the leftmost bound, once with the direction flipped (search right after a match) for the rightmost bound.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: Linear scan tracking the first and last index where nums[i] == target.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Two binary searches: one biased to find the leftmost match, one biased to find the rightmost match.

sections.title: Search in Rotated Sorted Array
sections.problemStatement: Given the array nums after a possible rotation and an integer target, return the index of target if it exists, or -1 otherwise, in O(log n) time.
constraints: nums is an ascending array, possibly rotated, all values distinct
hints: At every step, AT LEAST ONE half of the current range is genuinely sorted -- determine which half is sorted, then check if target falls within that half's range to decide which side to search.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: Linear scan for the target.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Modified binary search: identify which half (left or right of mid) is properly sorted, then decide which side to continue into based on whether target falls in that sorted half's range.

sections.title: Find Minimum in Rotated Sorted Array
sections.problemStatement: Given a rotated sorted array nums of unique elements, return the minimum element, in O(log n) time.
constraints: nums was originally sorted, then rotated between 1 and n times
hints: Compare nums[mid] to nums[right] -- if nums[mid] > nums[right], the minimum lies to the RIGHT of mid; otherwise it's at mid or to the LEFT.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: Scan the whole array tracking the minimum seen.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Binary search comparing nums[mid] to nums[right] to determine which half contains the rotation point (and thus the minimum).

sections.title: Koko Eating Bananas
sections.problemStatement: Koko has piles of bananas and h hours to eat them all. Each hour she chooses a pile and eats k bananas from it (or the whole pile if it has fewer than k). Return the minimum integer eating speed k such that she can eat all bananas within h hours.
hints: Binary search on the ANSWER (the eating speed k itself), not on the piles array -- this is a search over the range [1, max(piles)].
For a candidate speed k, compute the total hours needed; feasibility (hours <= h) is monotonic in k, which is exactly what makes binary search valid here.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: Try every possible speed from 1 upward, checking feasibility, until the smallest working speed is found.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Binary search on the eating speed itself, in the range [1, max(piles)], using feasibility (hours needed <= h) to decide direction.
